questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with itchy, red, dry patches on the cheeks and extensor surfaces of the arms and legs. The mother has a history of asthma. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The distribution (cheeks and extensor surfaces in an infant) and family history of atopy are classic for atopic eczema. Seborrhoeic dermatitis typically affects the scalp and flexures and is less pruritic.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child with known atopic eczema presents with a 2-day history of painful, crusted, vesicular lesions over the eczematous areas. The child is febrile and looks unwell. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of vesicles, punched-out erosions, and systemic symptoms in a patient with atopic eczema is pathognomonic for eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV). Impetigo is honey-coloured crusted but not vesicular/punched-out.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child with atopic eczema presents with worsening eczema, weeping, and golden-yellow crusts over the cheeks and arms. There is no fever. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The honey-coloured crusts and weeping suggest secondary bacterial infection with Staphylococcus aureus, which is common in atopic eczema. Eczema herpeticum has painful vesicles and systemic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child presents with an itchy rash on the cheeks and extensor surfaces of the arms. On examination, there is dry skin and mild flexural involvement in the popliteal fossae. According to the UK diagnostic criteria for atopic eczema, which of the following is an essential feature for diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The UK Working Party criteria require the presence of itch (pruritus) as a mandatory major criterion for the diagnosis of atopic eczema.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy with known atopic eczema develops a sudden onset of fever, malaise, and widespread painful vesicles, some with umbilication, on the eczematous skin. You suspect eczema herpeticum (Kaposi varicelliform eruption). What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: PCR for herpes simplex virus from a vesicle swab
Tzanck smear from a vesicle base
questions.explanation: PCR is the most sensitive and rapid test for HSV and is the preferred investigation to confirm eczema herpeticum, replacing viral culture in most UK centres.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a history of atopy presents with an itchy, scaly rash on the dorsum of both hands and wrists. She works as a hairdresser. You suspect possible allergic contact dermatitis superimposed on atopic eczema. Which investigation is most useful to differentiate these?
questions.explanation: Patch testing is the gold standard for diagnosing allergic contact dermatitis and can distinguish it from atopic eczema.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with dry skin and an itchy rash affecting the cheeks and extensor surfaces. According to the UK diagnostic criteria for atopic eczema in children under 2 years, which of the following is a minor feature supporting the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Onset under 2 years is one of the minor criteria in the UK diagnostic criteria for atopic eczema in children; flexural involvement may not be seen until after infancy.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with long-standing atopic eczema presents with diffuse erythema and scaling affecting >90% of his body surface area. He has no fever or lymphadenopathy. You suspect erythrodermic eczema, but psoriasis and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma are differentials. Which investigation would be most helpful in confirming the underlying diagnosis?
options: Skin biopsy from a representative area
questions.explanation: Skin biopsy can provide histopathological features that differentiate erythrodermic eczema (spongiosis) from psoriasis (psoriasiform hyperplasia) or cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (atypical lymphocytes).

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child presents with dry, itchy patches on the flexures of the elbows and knees. There is no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For mild eczema, first-line treatment is emollients plus a mild potency topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%). Potent steroids (betamethasone) are reserved for more severe cases. Antihistamines are not first-line. Tacrolimus is second-line for moderate disease.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with known atopic eczema presents with a 2-day history of painful, crusted vesicles over her face and neck. She is febrile. On examination, there are punched-out ulcers and crusted lesions. What treatment is contraindicated in this patient?
questions.explanation: In eczema herpeticum, topical corticosteroids are contraindicated as they exacerbate viral infection. Systemic acyclovir is the mainstay. Emollients and paracetamol are supportive. Antibiotics are used if secondary bacterial infection is suspected.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with moderate atopic eczema has been using clobetasone butyrate 0.05% ointment (moderate potency) for body and hydrocortisone for face intermittently. Despite this, he has frequent flares requiring potent topical steroids (betamethasone valerate 0.1%). He has no contraindications. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue with emollients and as-needed potent topical steroids
Add topical pimecrolimus 1% cream for affected areas
Start oral prednisolone 30 mg daily for 2 weeks
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a topical calcineurin inhibitor (pimecrolimus or tacrolimus) as second-line for moderate eczema not controlled with potent topical steroids. Phototherapy and systemic agents are reserved for severe disease or after failure of topical calcineurin inhibitors.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old man with severe atopic eczema presents with acute generalized erythema and exudative lesions. He is unable to apply topical treatments due to widespread involvement. He has no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate first-line systemic treatment for short-term control?
options: Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 1 week
questions.explanation: For acute severe flares requiring rapid control, a short course of oral prednisolone is first-line. Ciclosporin, methotrexate, azathioprine are steroid-sparing agents with slower onset. Dupilumab is reserved for chronic moderate-to-severe disease after systemic therapy failure.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child presents with dry, itchy patches on the cheeks and flexures for the first time. There is no weeping or crusting. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to dermatology for patch testing
questions.explanation: Emollients are first-line for all severities of atopic eczema. Topical steroids are added if emollients alone are insufficient; this is a new presentation with mild disease, so starting with emollients is correct.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with atopic eczema has used emollients and a mild topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for weeks with only partial improvement. She has areas of lichenification on the wrists and popliteal fossae. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start a tapering course of oral prednisolone
Switch to a potent topical corticosteroid (e.g., betamethasone valerate 0.1%)
questions.explanation: After failure of mild potency steroid, the next step is a short burst of a potent topical corticosteroid. NICE recommends stepping up potency until control is achieved. Phototherapy or oral steroids are third-line options.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child with known atopic eczema presents with a 2-day history of rapidly spreading blisters and erosions, fever of 39°C, and feeling unwell. There is no crusting. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain a skin swab for culture
questions.explanation: The presentation is classic for eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV). This is a dermatological emergency requiring urgent IV aciclovir. Antibiotics may be needed later if bacterial co-infection is suspected, but antivirals take precedence.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child with atopic eczema has widespread weeping, crusted patches and a temperature of 38.5°C. There is no blistering. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain a swab for culture and sensitivity
Increase the potency of topical corticosteroid
questions.explanation: Signs of bacterial superinfection with systemic symptoms (fever) require oral antibiotics. Flucloxacillin is first-line for staphylococcal infection. Topical antibiotics are insufficient for widespread infection, and IV antibiotics are not needed unless oral fails or severe sepsis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (16 weeks gestation) with mild atopic eczema is currently using emollients and occasional mild topical steroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for flare-ups. She asks what she should continue. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Advise to stop all topical steroids
Switch to a topical calcineurin inhibitor
Advise to use a moderate topical steroid as needed
questions.explanation: Emollients are safe in pregnancy and should be continued. Topical steroids, especially mild ones, are also considered safe but the question asks for the immediate next step; the priority is to advise on the mainstay of maintenance therapy, which is emollients.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with atopic eczema presents with a new vesicular rash on the face and trunk. Which finding is most suggestive of eczema herpeticum?
questions.explanation: Eczema herpeticum is characterised by monomorphic, punched-out vesicles that are often umbilicated. Pustules with honey-coloured crust suggest impetigo; fever and lymphadenopathy are non-specific. The presence of typical vesicles is the key discriminator.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old with long-standing atopic eczema presents with diffuse, confluent erythema covering over 90% of the body surface area, along with malaise and lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely complication?
questions.explanation: Erythroderma is defined as diffuse erythema affecting >90% BSA and is a dermatological emergency often triggered by severe eczema. While lymphoma can mimic, the history of eczema makes erythroderma the most likely.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old with atopic eczema develops sudden, painful erythema with superficial blisters and a positive Nikolsky sign. Which life-threatening condition must be suspected?
questions.explanation: Positive Nikolsky sign and superficial blisters in a child with eczema point to staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, caused by exfoliative toxins. Eczema herpeticum has vesicles but not the widespread erythema and Nikolsky sign.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman with atopic eczema develops sudden widespread blistering lesions, starting around the umbilicus. Which condition should be excluded?
questions.explanation: Pemphigoid gestationis is an autoimmune blistering disease of pregnancy that can mimic eczema. Blisters starting periumbilically are characteristic. Impetigo herpetiformis is pustular psoriasis, not typically blistering.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old with atopic eczema presents with sudden severe eye pain, photophobia, and blurred vision. Which complication must be considered?
questions.explanation: Herpetic keratitis is a sight-threatening emergency in atopic patients due to impaired corneal immunity. Conjunctivitis and blepharitis are common but do not cause severe pain or photophobia; uveitis is less likely without other features.
